topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished mahogany of the office gleamed under the soft, indirect lighting. I stood awkwardly by the reception desk, clutching a lukewarm coffee cup as if it were a shield. Dr. Elias Vance, once the architect of my most miserable middle school years—the one whose shadow loomed over every recess—now possessed an aura of serene, practiced calm. He was wearing a tailored charcoal suit, his silver hair impeccably swept back, and his eyes, those same sharp, assessing eyes that used to sneer at me, held a gentle, unwavering focus. When he looked up, catching my gaze, a flicker—something unreadable—crossed his face before he offered a small, almost apologetic smile. My breath hitched in my throat. It felt like the floor had tilted beneath me. "Alex," he greeted, his voice a low, resonant baritone that was entirely unfamiliar, "please, have a seat. I've been looking forward to our session." I managed a jerky nod, my hands suddenly damp.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the quiet dignity of the setting to feel so profoundly jarring. The room smelled faintly of sandalwood and old paper, a stark contrast to the stale gymnasium air I associated with him. When he finally turned around, the transformation was startling. The scowl, the perpetual smirk that had been his trademark for years, was entirely absent. In its place was an expression of profound, almost weary empathy. I froze mid-step, my feet rooted to the expensive carpet. My palms began to sweat, and I instinctively shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a nervous tic I hadn't realized I possessed. "Hi," I managed, the sound thin and reedy. He inclined his head, a gesture so refined it felt mocking. "Hello, Alex. Take a moment. We have a lot to unpack." The words sounded like a carefully constructed trap, and I found myself gripping my own arms tightly across my chest, wishing I could dissolve into the expensive upholstery.

[story 3]

It was the familiarity of his posture that truly unsettled me. The way he held himself—straight, composed, radiating an authority that felt entirely different from the aggressive dominance I remembered. He was sitting behind a vast desk, reviewing a file, and when he finally looked up, his gaze swept over me with an unnerving thoroughness. My stomach performed a violent flip, a sickening lurch that made my vision swim for a second. I tried to pull my shoulders back, attempting to mimic the defiant stance I used to adopt in the hallways, but my muscles felt sluggish, unresponsive. "You look like you've seen a ghost," he observed, his tone devoid of mockery, merely observational. I swallowed hard, my throat constricting. "No," I whispered, the word barely audible. I stared fixedly at the knot in the wood of the desk, trying desperately to anchor myself to reality, because the reality standing before me was fundamentally rearranged.
